title: The Absolute Basics of Ratios and Proportions
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You've seen ratios. You've seen proportions. They sound fancy, but they're really just comparisons. Once you understand what they're saying, solving them becomes almost automatic. Here's how.

A Ratio Is Just a Comparison

If a class has 10 boys and 15 girls, the ratio of boys to girls is 10:15. That's it. You're just comparing two numbers. Ratios can be written three ways:

• 10:15 (with a colon)
• 10 to 15 (with the word 'to')
• 10/15 (as a fraction)

They all mean the same thing. Pick whichever format your teacher prefers.

Simplify Ratios Like Fractions

Just like you simplify fractions, you can simplify ratios. 10:15 simplifies to 2:3 (divide both by 5). A simplified ratio tells you the relationship without the extra numbers. For every 2 boys, there are 3 girls.

Always simplify your ratios unless your teacher says otherwise. It makes them easier to understand and compare.

A Proportion Is Two Equal Ratios

A proportion says that two ratios are equal. 2/3 = 10/15 is a proportion. It's saying '2 is to 3 as 10 is to 15.'

Proportions are useful because you can solve for a missing number. If you know 2/3 = x/15, you can find x. That's where the real power comes in.

How to Solve Proportions (Cross-Multiply)

This is the method you need to memorize. For the proportion a/b = c/d, cross-multiply: a × d = b × c. Then solve for the missing number.

Example: 2/3 = x/15
Step 1: Cross-multiply: 2 × 15 = 3 × x
Step 2: 30 = 3x
Step 3: Divide both sides by 3: x = 10

That's it. Check: 2/3 equals 10/15 (which simplifies to 2/3). Correct.

This works every time. Memorize it. Practice it. It will show up on tests for years.

Real-Life Examples (Where You'll Actually Use This)

Scaling a recipe: A recipe for 4 people needs 2 cups of flour. How much flour for 10 people? 2/4 = x/10 → 2 × 10 = 4 × x → 20 = 4x → x = 5 cups.

Unit price: A 12-pack of soda costs $6. How much does one soda cost? $6/12 = x/1 → 6 × 1 = 12 × x → 6 = 12x → x = $0.50 per soda.

Map scale: A map says 1 inch = 5 miles. Two cities are 3 inches apart on the map. How far apart are they? 1/5 = 3/x → 1 × x = 5 × 3 → x = 15 miles.

Speed: You drive 120 miles in 2 hours. At the same speed, how far in 5 hours? 120/2 = x/5 → 120 × 5 = 2 × x → 600 = 2x → x = 300 miles.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Mistake 1: Forgetting to keep units consistent. If one ratio uses minutes, the other must use minutes. Don't mix minutes and hours.

Mistake 2: Cross-multiplying in the wrong order. Remember: multiply across the equal sign, not up and down. a/b = c/d → a × d = b × c.

Mistake 3: Not simplifying at the end. If your answer is 10/2, simplify to 5. Always check if you can reduce.

Practice This One Weird Trick

When you see a proportion problem, write down what you know. Identify the two ratios. One will have a missing number. Set up the proportion. Cross-multiply. Solve. Check your work.

Do this five times, and you'll never get a proportion wrong again. It's that consistent.

Ratios and proportions are everywhere — from cooking to shopping to driving to science. Once you master them, you've mastered one of the most useful math skills for everyday life.
